Sample Rides

A sample ride is a ride you've actually done — whether you recorded it in PackTimes or entered it by hand. PackTimes compares it against its baseline and works out whether you're faster or slower than an average rider. That's the number above.

It keeps up to 20 rides, covering different distances, surfaces and loads. As newer rides come in, it drops older ones that are very similar — so you end up with a spread of the different kinds of riding you do, rather than twenty versions of the same one.

How to choose a good sample ride

Ride it like the event you're planning

Steady all-day endurance pace, long enough to be honest. Don't use a flat-out effort or a PB — a short maximum effort makes every plan optimistic.

Keep rides to a single surface as much as possible

Sealed, gravel, singletrack or rough track — whichever it mostly was. A pure single-surface ride is rare, so don't agonise: pick a section of a longer ride if you can, since shorter sections make one surface more likely.

Say what shape the section was

Rolling/loop if it finishes near the height it started, or mostly-climbing for a climb. Avoid mostly-downhill sections — they measure your braking, not your fitness.

For time, include the faff

Use elapsed time minus your deliberate breaks (meals, sleep, long resupply stops) — but keep the short stops in. Layers, snacks, bottles, gates: that routine faff is part of real bikepacking, and counting it here is what makes your ETAs match when you actually arrive. Recorded rides do this automatically — stops under 15 minutes count as ride time, longer breaks are excluded.

Using a Strava segment or a non-stop effort?

Segments are usually short — a good climb, a clean stretch of bitumen — and ridden straight through, so there's no faff in them at all. Tick "Add 15% faff" on that sample. Without it the sample looks faster than you really are on a long day, and every plan you make comes out optimistic. Same goes if all you have is Strava's "moving time", which hides your stops.
